questions.question: Explain the Linear Search algorithm with its time and space complexity.
keyPoints: Linear Search examines each element sequentially from the beginning until target is found or list ends
Worst case time complexity: O(n) - checks all n elements
Best case time complexity: O(1) - element found at first position
Average case time complexity: O(n)
Space complexity: O(1) for iterative implementation

questions.question: When is Linear Search preferred over Binary Search? Give at least three scenarios.
keyPoints: When the array is unsorted - Binary Search requires sorted data
For small arrays (n < 20-30) where overhead of sorting isn't justified
When only single search operation is needed
When the data structure doesn't support random access (like linked lists)
When implementation simplicity is more important than efficiency

questions.question: Write an algorithm for Linear Search in pseudocode and explain each step.
keyPoints: Input: array A[0...n-1] and target element x
Initialize index i = 0
Repeat while i < n: check if A[i] == x, if yes return i, else increment i
If loop completes, return -1 (element not found)
Each iteration checks one element sequentially

questions.question: What is Sentinel Linear Search? How is it different from basic Linear Search?
keyPoints: Sentinel Linear Search adds the target element at the end of the array
This eliminates the boundary check (i < n) in each iteration
Reduces one comparison per iteration
The loop terminates when either target is found or sentinel is reached
Slight performance improvement but modifies the original array

questions.question: Differentiate between best case, worst case, and average case analysis of Linear Search.
keyPoints: Best Case: Element at first position - 1 comparison, O(1) time
Worst Case: Element at last position or not present - n comparisons, O(n) time
Average Case: Element equally likely at any position - (n+1)/2 comparisons, O(n) time
Average case assumes uniform distribution of target position
Worst case is important for guaranteed performance bounds

questions.question: Can Linear Search be applied to sorted arrays? If yes, how can it be optimized?
keyPoints: Yes, Linear Search works on sorted arrays but is inefficient
Optimization: Stop searching when current element exceeds target in sorted (ascending) array
This early termination reduces average comparisons for unsuccessful searches
For sorted arrays, still O(n) worst case but can be faster in practice
Binary Search O(log n) is still preferred for sorted arrays

questions.question: What are the advantages and disadvantages of Linear Search?
keyPoints: Advantages: Simple to understand and implement, works on unsorted data, minimal memory requirements, versatile for various data structures
Disadvantages: O(n) time complexity is slow for large datasets, no leverage of data ordering, inefficient for frequent searches
Not suitable for large databases or time-critical applications

questions.question: Write a C program to implement Linear Search. The program should accept an array of integers from the user, accept the element to search, and display its position if found or an appropriate message if not found.
keyPoints: Include necessary header files (stdio.h)
Define main function and declare variables (array, n, key, position, i)
Accept array size and elements from user using scanf
Accept the search key from user
Implement Linear Search using for/while loop
Check each element against key, return index if found
If loop completes without finding, return -1
Display appropriate message based on result
Code should compile without errors

questions.question: Compare Linear Search and Binary Search. Under what circumstances would you choose one over the other? Provide justification with time complexity analysis.
keyPoints: Linear Search: O(n) time, O(1) space, works on unsorted data, simple implementation
Binary Search: O(log n) time, O(1) space (iterative), requires sorted data, slightly complex
Choose Linear Search when: data unsorted, small dataset, single search, linked list
Choose Binary Search when: data sorted, large dataset, frequent searches
Trade-off: Binary Search requires O(n log n) for sorting if data unsorted
For static sorted data with frequent searches, Binary Search is clearly superior

questions.question: Write a recursive C function for Linear Search. Explain the base case and recursive case. Also discuss the space complexity of recursive Linear Search.
keyPoints: Function signature: int recursiveLinearSearch(int arr[], int n, int key, int index)
Base case: if index == n, return -1 (element not found)
Base case: if arr[index] == key, return index (element found)
Recursive case: return recursiveLinearSearch(arr, n, key, index+1)
Initial call made with index = 0
Space complexity: O(n) due to n recursive calls on stack
Each recursive call adds stack frame with return address and parameters
Iterative version with O(1) space is generally preferred
Recursion adds function call overhead affecting performance

questions.question: A university maintains a database of student records stored in an unsorted array. Each record contains student ID and name. Explain how you would search for a student by ID. Discuss the time complexity and suggest improvements if searches are frequent.
keyPoints: Current scenario: Array is unsorted, so Linear Search is the only option
Time complexity: O(n) per search for n student records
For m searches: O(mn) total time
If searches are infrequent: Linear Search is acceptable
If searches are frequent: Sort the array by student ID first (O(n log n))
After sorting: Use Binary Search (O(log n) per search)
For m searches after sorting: O(n log n + m log n) = O((n+m) log n)
Alternative: Use hash table for O(1) average search time
Trade-off: Additional memory and complexity for hash table
